summary: Added a `-l` option to the repository root `build.sh` helper so build output can be appended to `build.log` through `tee`, enabling workflows like `tail -f build.log` while preserving normal console output. The script was lightly refactored by wrapping the existing build/clean/rebuild/expected-sync flow in a `build()` function and adding timestamp messages at build start and finish.
what_changed: Introduced a `log=false` flag and documented `-l  log: tee output to build.log` in the `build.sh` usage text.
Extended `getopts` from `:cr:emnfxh` to `:cr:emnfxhl` and added an `l) log=true ;;` case.
Wrapped the previous main build logic in a `build()` shell function so the entire operation can be piped through `tee` when logging is enabled.
Added a `build_time()` helper using `date +%T.%N` and emitted `Build started at ...` and `Build finished at ...` messages.
When `-l` is passed, runs `build | tee -a build.log`; otherwise runs `build` directly.
Kept existing clean, rebuild-pattern deletion, make invocation, and expected-build syncing behavior inside the new `build()` function.
Quoted `pushd "$HERE"`, improving shell robustness for paths with spaces compared with the previous unquoted `pushd $HERE`.
smart_moves: Piping a single `build()` function through `tee` avoided duplicating logging logic around each individual `make`, `find`, and `rsync` step.
Using `tee -a build.log` preserves interactive terminal output while appending a persistent log for later inspection.
The PR body explicitly documented the intended monitoring workflow: `tail -f build.log`.
The refactor kept the existing build result handling local to the build flow, so expected-output syncing still depends on the `make` exit code.
Adding start and finish timestamps makes long build logs easier to correlate with rebuild attempts.
decomp_lessons: Small repository tooling improvements can materially help decomp iteration by making repeated builds observable without losing terminal output.
For shell build helpers, wrap the main workflow in a function before adding optional full-stream behavior such as logging; this keeps the non-logged path and logged path equivalent.
Append-only build logs plus `tail -f` are useful for long-running matching or rebuild sessions where developers may want a persistent record.
When touching shell scripts, opportunistic quoting of path variables such as `"$HERE"` improves portability without changing intended behavior.
naming_conventions: Command-line flags in `build.sh` use lowercase booleans such as `clean`, `expected`, `clear`, and the new `log`.
Make variables passed by the script remain uppercase: `NON_MATCHING`, `GENERATE_MAP`, and `EPILOGUE_PROCESS`.
The log file name introduced by this PR is the simple repository-root filename `build.log`.
Helper functions added in the script use lowercase snake_case names, e.g. `build_time`.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: No direct assembly matching or C decompilation tactics were present; this PR only changes build script ergonomics.
Indirectly supports matching work by making build output easier to monitor and archive during iterative rebuilds.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or formal reviews were included in the provided PR context.
The PR was merged quickly, but the dump does not provide reviewer rationale.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that modify `build.sh` logging behavior or `build.log` handling.
Search for whether `build.log` was added to `.gitignore` or otherwise documented after this PR.
Look for subsequent build helper refactors that reused the `build()` function pattern.
Check if later tooling standardized timestamp formatting for build logs.
